---
title: "4.15 Sorting Algorithms"
subtitle: "Selection Sort and Insertion Sort"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Sorting Algorithms

### AP CSA sorting algorithms

There are many sorting algorithms to put array or `ArrayList` elements in alphabetic or numerical order.

Selection sort and insertion sort are iterative sorting algorithms that can be used to sort elements in an array or `ArrayList`.

The three sorting algorithms you need to know for the AP CSA exam are:

- Selection sort
- Insertion sort
- Merge sort, covered in lesson 4.17

## Selection Sort

### Find min and swap

Selection sort usually starts at index `0`.

It looks through the entire array keeping track of the index of the smallest value, then swaps the value at the smallest index with the value at index `0`.

Then it does the same thing for index `1`, then `2`, and so on until it reaches the length of the array minus one.

## Identify Selection Sort

### Source recognition points

To identify a selection sort, look for:

- an outer loop that starts at `0` and ends at `elements.length - 1`
- setting `minIndex` to the outer loop index
- an inner loop that starts at the outer loop index plus `1`
- comparing the inner-loop value to the current minimum
- saving the new minimum index
- swapping values after the inner loop

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: selSort`

The code for `selectionSort` below is from the AP CSA course description.

## Selection Sort Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.Arrays;

public class SortTest
{
    public static void selectionSort(int[] elements)
    {
        for (int j = 0; j < elements.length - 1; j++)
        {
            int minIndex = j;
            for (int k = j + 1; k < elements.length; k++)
            {
                if (elements[k] < elements[minIndex])
                {
                    minIndex = k;
                }
            }
            int temp = elements[j];
            elements[j] = elements[minIndex];
            elements[minIndex] = temp;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] arr1 = {3, 86, -20, 14, 40};
        System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr1));
        selectionSort(arr1);
        System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr1));
    }
}
```
:::

## Selection Sort Test Target

### Runestone expected output

```text
[3, 86, -20, 14, 40]
[-20, 3, 14, 40, 86]
```

## Selection Sort Quick Checks

### `qsel_1` and `qsel_2`

Under what condition will a selection sort execute faster?

Correct answer: **It will always take the same amount of time to execute.**

In the broken selection sort method, the wrong line is **line 3** because the inner loop should start at the outer loop index plus `1`.

## Insertion Sort

### Insert into the sorted part

Insertion sort usually starts at index `1`.

It inserts the value at index `1` into its correct place in the already sorted part to the left of the current index.

It moves any value larger than `temp` to the right until it finds the appropriate place to put `temp` or reaches the front of the array.

## Identify Insertion Sort

### Source recognition points

To identify an insertion sort, look for:

- an outer `for` loop that starts at `1`
- storing the element at the outer loop index in `temp`
- setting `possibleIndex` to the outer loop index
- an inner `while` loop that shifts larger values right
- decrementing `possibleIndex`
- setting `elements[possibleIndex]` to `temp`

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: insertionSort`

The code for `insertionSort` below is from the AP CSA course description.

## Insertion Sort Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.Arrays;

public class SortTest
{
    public static void insertionSort(int[] elements)
    {
        for (int j = 1; j < elements.length; j++)
        {
            int temp = elements[j];
            int possibleIndex = j;
            while (possibleIndex > 0 && temp < elements[possibleIndex - 1])
            {
                elements[possibleIndex] = elements[possibleIndex - 1];
                possibleIndex--;
            }
            elements[possibleIndex] = temp;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] arr1 = {3, 86, -20, 14, 40};
        System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr1));
        insertionSort(arr1);
        System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr1));
    }
}
```
:::

## Insertion Sort Test Target

### Runestone expected output

```text
[3, 86, -20, 14, 40]
[-20, 3, 14, 40, 86]
```

## Insertion Sort Quick Checks

### `qins_1` and `qins_2`

Under what condition will an insertion sort execute faster?

Correct answer: **If the data is already sorted in ascending order.**

In the broken insertion sort method, the wrong line is **line 1** because it should loop through the entire array.

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### Sort Runtimes

Selection sort and insertion sort have similar runtimes.

They both have nested loops that run through data of size `n` approximately `n` squared times.

However, they perform differently on some data.

## Student Response

### `shortanswer:: challenge-sorting`

In the Active code windows for Selection sort and Insertion sort above:

1. Add a counter.
2. Increment it inside the inner loop to count iterations.
3. Add print statements that print the counter value after the loops.
4. Run the code on the same data.
5. Compare the runtimes of selection and insertion sort.

Explain why one performed better on some data.

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should include

- identify selection sort by finding the minimum and swapping after the inner loop
- identify insertion sort by shifting larger values right and inserting `temp`
- explain why selection sort does the same number of comparisons
- explain why insertion sort can be faster on already sorted data
- preserve the AP CSA source code structure for both algorithms

## End

### 4.15 Sorting Algorithms

Next: Recursion.
